Figure 1: Average closing bid-ask spreads of options. Panels A and B show the average closing relative bid-ask spreads of options written on stocks in the S&P 500. Panels C and D show corresponding values for options written on the S&P 500 Index. Panels A and C show the results for the baseline sample, while Panels B and D show the results for ATM options with 21 to 23 trading days to maturity. We classify all options with|־”|$|\Delta |$between 0.45 and 0.55 as ATM options. Both equal-weighted and dollar volume ($VOL$\$VOL$) weighted averages are presented. Option spreads are in percentages relative to quote midpoints. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

Figure 3: Average volatility smile and integrand function used to estimate the model-free implied variance (MFIV$MFIV$). Panel A shows the averages in our sample of the implied volatilities of out-of-the-money options used to estimate theMFIVs$MFIV{\rm s}$in TableXIII. The circles (squares) are average implied volatilities of out-of-the-money puts (calls) from IvyDB. The lines are the average interpolated implied volatilities used to numerically calculate theMFIV$MFIV$by an integral of a function of option prices on strike prices. Panel B shows the average integrand used in theMFIV$MFIV$estimation. The implied volatility and integrand are plotted as a function of the ratio of strike-to-spot price (K/S$K/S$). The dotted lines in these figuresֲ represent the volatility smile and integrand used to estimate theMFIV$MFIV$in the the sample with deep OTM options in TableXIII. The continuous lines represent the volatility smile and integrand used in the estimation of theMFIV$MFIV$in the sample without deep OTM options in TableXIII. The continuous line in Panel A keeps implied volatility constant for OTM put options with־”P>גˆ’0.05$\Delta ^P>-0.05$and OTM calls with־”C<0.15$\Delta ^C<0.15$resulting in lower integrand values in Panel B.
